h1Title: Acute Cholecystitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute cholecystitis is sudden inflammation of the gallbladder, occurring when a gallstone obstructs the cystic duct in approximately 90-95% of cases (calculous cholecystitis) or from ischemia and bile stasis without stones in critically ill patients (acalculous cholecystitis). Gallstone disease affects approximately 10-15% of adults in Western countries, and approximately 1-3% of those with gallstones develop acute cholecystitis per year. Right upper quadrant pain, fever, and a positive Murphy's sign are the cardinal features. The Tokyo Guidelines (TG18), updated in 2018, are the international standard for diagnosis and severity grading. Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72 hours of symptom onset — supported by level 1 evidence — reduces complications and hospital stay compared with delayed surgery.

keyStats.prevalence: Gallstone disease affects 10-15% of adults in Western countries; approximately 600,000 cholecystectomies are performed annually in the US, with acute cholecystitis accounting for approximately 20% of these
keyStats.demographics: Women are affected 2-3 times more frequently than men; risk increases sharply after age 40; obesity, multiparity, rapid weight loss, and hormone use are major modifiable risk factors
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age approximately 50-55 years; sharp increase in incidence after age 40 particularly in women
keyStats.globalCases: Cholecystitis is among the top 10 causes of acute abdominal surgery globally; gallstone prevalence varies from 5-10% in East Asian populations to 70-80% in Pima Native Americans

definition: Acute cholecystitis (ICD-10: K81.0) is acute inflammatory disease of the gallbladder, most commonly caused by obstruction of the cystic duct by a gallstone, leading to gallbladder wall distension, ischemia, secondary bacterial infection, and potential progression to gangrene or perforation. In calculous cholecystitis — accounting for 90-95% of cases — persistent cystic duct obstruction raises intraluminal pressure, impairing venous and lymphatic outflow, which promotes mucosal ischemia and subsequent invasion by enteric bacteria, primarily Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Enterococcus species. Acalculous cholecystitis, accounting for the remaining 5-10%, occurs in critically ill patients — typically those in the ICU following major trauma, burns, post-cardiac surgery, or with prolonged parenteral nutrition — through gallbladder stasis, bile concentration, and ischemia. The Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18) classify acute cholecystitis by severity: Grade I (mild, no organ dysfunction, resolves with antibiotics and early surgery), Grade II (moderate, involving local complications), and Grade III (severe, with organ dysfunction requiring ICU care). Management involves gallbladder decompression — primarily by laparoscopic cholecystectomy — and antibiotic therapy; the specialty most involved is general surgery, with gastroenterology and interventional radiology providing complementary care.

typesClassification.description: Cystic duct obstruction by gallstone without organ dysfunction or local complications. Responds well to IV antibiotics; most suitable for early laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72 hours. In-hospital mortality below 0.5%.

Local complications present: pericholecystic abscess, peritonitis limited to the right upper quadrant, biliary-enteric fistula, or emphysematous cholecystitis. Requires early surgical or interventional management; higher conversion rate to open surgery.

Multi-organ dysfunction present (cardiovascular, neurological, respiratory, renal, hepatic, or hematological). Highest mortality (approximately 6-18%); initial gallbladder drainage by percutaneous cholecystostomy followed by interval cholecystectomy when stable is preferred over emergency surgery in poor surgical candidates.

Develops in critically ill patients without gallstones due to bile stasis, ischemia, and bacterial overgrowth. Typically diagnosed by ultrasound in the ICU. Carries higher mortality (30-50%) than calculous disease because of the severity of the underlying illness. Treated by percutaneous cholecystostomy in most cases.

Gas-forming bacterial infection of the gallbladder wall (Clostridium welchii, E. coli), seen predominantly in elderly men and diabetics. CT shows gas within the gallbladder wall. Gangrenous and perforated in most cases; emergency cholecystectomy required. Mortality 15-25%.

primarySymptoms: Sudden-onset right upper quadrant pain — the cardinal symptom — typically severe, constant (unlike the colicky intermittent pain of simple biliary colic), and persisting more than 6 hours without relief.
Radiation of pain to the right shoulder tip or right scapula from diaphragmatic irritation via the phrenic nerve — present in approximately 50% of cases.
Fever (typically 38-39°C) from the inflammatory response and secondary bacterial infection; temperatures above 39°C suggest complications (empyema, gangrene, perforation).
Nausea and vomiting — present in approximately 70-80% of patients; retching without relief after the acute onset of pain is characteristic.

Murphy's sign — sudden inspiratory arrest caused by pain when the examiner's fingers contact the inflamed gallbladder beneath the right costal margin during inspiration — the most specific clinical sign, with a specificity of approximately 97% but sensitivity of only 65%.
Right upper quadrant guarding and tenderness on palpation; tenderness may extend across the right hypochondrium.
Palpable, tender right upper quadrant mass in approximately 20-30% of patients, representing the distended gallbladder with surrounding omentum (the 'inflammatory mass').
Mild jaundice (scleral icterus) in approximately 10-15% of cases from concomitant choledocholithiasis (common bile duct stones) or external compression of the common bile duct.
Anorexia and inability to tolerate fatty foods, particularly preceding the acute episode.
Bloating, epigastric fullness, and postprandial dyspepsia — prodromal symptoms common in patients with gallstones before the acute episode.
earlyWarningSigns: Recurrent episodes of right upper quadrant or epigastric pain after fatty meals lasting 30 minutes to 6 hours then completely resolving — biliary colic preceding acute cholecystitis, present in the majority of patients.
Intolerance of fatty or fried foods with bloating and dyspepsia in a patient with known gallstones — signals worsening biliary pathology.
Fever developing alongside the typical RUQ pain of a biliary colic episode — indicates transition from uncomplicated colic to inflammatory cholecystitis.
Mildly elevated liver enzymes (ALT, AST, bilirubin) on routine blood work in a patient with RUQ discomfort — may reflect biliary obstruction from stones not yet causing acute inflammation.

Pain persisting beyond 6 hours in a patient with known gallstones who typically has short-duration colicky episodes — the duration beyond 6 hours strongly predicts cholecystitis rather than simple biliary colic.
emergencySigns: High fever above 39.5°C combined with rigors and right upper quadrant pain — suggests empyema (pus-filled gallbladder), ascending cholangitis, or gangrenous cholecystitis requiring emergency intervention.
Sudden worsening of RUQ pain with generalized abdominal tenderness, guarding, and rebound — free perforation of the gallbladder causing bile peritonitis; requires emergency surgery.
Jaundice with RUQ pain and high fever (Charcot's triad) — the hallmark of acute ascending cholangitis, a separate but related biliary emergency requiring urgent ERCP and biliary decompression.

Hypotension and tachycardia with a distended, rigid abdomen in a patient with known or suspected cholecystitis — septic shock requiring immediate fluid resuscitation, IV antibiotics, and emergency surgery.
Palpable tender RUQ mass with fever not responding to 24-48 hours of IV antibiotics — likely pericholecystic abscess or empyema requiring urgent imaging and drainage.

causes.cause: Gallstone impaction in the cystic duct
causes.description: The dominant cause in 90-95% of acute cholecystitis. A gallstone (cholesterol or mixed stone) migrates from the gallbladder into the cystic duct and becomes impacted, raising intragallbladder pressure. Sustained pressure exceeding 20 cmH2O triggers mucosal ischemia, bile concentration, and chemical irritation of the gallbladder wall, which then recruits neutrophils and secondary bacterial infection within hours to days.

In critically ill patients without gallstones, prolonged fasting (especially on total parenteral nutrition), major surgery, severe burns, trauma, or prolonged critical illness reduces the cholecystokinin-mediated gallbladder contraction that normally empties bile. Concentrated bile and gallbladder ischemia (from systemic hypotension, vasopressors, or splanchnic hypoperfusion) produce the same inflammatory cascade as a stone obstruction.

Once gallbladder wall integrity is disrupted, intestinal bacteria colonize the bile. Escherichia coli is the most common organism (50-60%), followed by Klebsiella pneumoniae, Enterococcus faecalis, Bacteroides fragilis, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Polymicrobial infection is present in approximately 20-30% of cases. Bacterial cultures of bile obtained at surgery guide antibiotic de-escalation.

Biliary sludge — a mixture of calcium bilirubinate crystals and cholesterol monohydrate suspended in mucin — can accumulate in the gallbladder and obstruct the cystic duct in much the same way as a macroscopic gallstone. Sludge is particularly common during pregnancy, rapid weight loss, and total parenteral nutrition and can precipitate acute cholecystitis without a discrete stone visible on ultrasound.

causes.cause: Parasitic infection and bile duct worms
causes.description: In endemic regions, Ascaris lumbricoides migrating into the biliary tree, and Clonorchis sinensis or Opisthorchis spp. colonizing the bile ducts, can obstruct the cystic duct and precipitate acute cholecystitis. This mechanism is important in South, Southeast, and East Asia and in immigrants from these regions presenting with unexplained biliary disease.

riskFactors.description: Gallstones are the substrate for 90-95% of acute cholecystitis. Risk of developing cholecystitis is approximately 1-2% per year in asymptomatic gallstone carriers, rising with stone size (stones >3 cm confer higher risk) and with prior biliary symptoms.

Women are 2-3 times more likely than men to develop gallstones and cholecystitis; estrogen increases biliary cholesterol saturation and progesterone reduces gallbladder motility. Each pregnancy further increases risk. The classic 'four F' mnemonic (fat, female, fertile, forty) reflects this epidemiology.

Obesity increases cholesterol secretion into bile, raising saturation and stone formation risk approximately 3-fold at BMI 40+ compared with normal weight. Rapid weight loss (>1.5 kg/week) following bariatric surgery or caloric restriction also precipitates stone formation in 30-40% of patients.

Gallstone prevalence increases with age: approximately 4% at age 30, rising to 30-40% by age 70 in Western populations. Acute cholecystitis in the elderly carries higher complication rates (gangrene, perforation, mortality) from delayed presentation and blunted inflammatory response.

Diabetes increases gallbladder dysmotility, gallstone formation, and risk of acalculous and emphysematous cholecystitis. Diabetic patients have higher rates of gangrenous cholecystitis and perforation than non-diabetics — approximately 2-fold higher in case series.

Acalculous cholecystitis affects approximately 0.5-1.5% of critically ill ICU patients on total parenteral nutrition or after major surgery. The condition carries 30-50% mortality from the severity of the underlying disease, not the biliary complication alone.

riskFactors.factor: Rapid weight loss or bariatric surgery
riskFactors.description: Weight loss faster than 1.5 kg per week — whether from bariatric surgery, caloric restriction, or illness — promotes gallstone formation in 30-40% of cases. Ursodeoxycholic acid 500-600 mg/day for 6 months following bariatric surgery reduces the formation of new stones by approximately 40%.

affectedDemographics: Women aged 40-70 have the highest incidence of calculous acute cholecystitis; the female-to-male ratio is approximately 2.5:1 in most Western series.
Older adults (>65 years) have higher rates of gangrenous and perforated cholecystitis (approximately 20-30% of older presentations) because of blunted fever response and delayed diagnosis.
Pima Native Americans have the highest reported gallstone prevalence globally (70-80%), reflecting specific genetic risk for cholesterol gallstone formation.
ICU patients on TPN or post-major surgery: acalculous cholecystitis prevalence approximately 0.5-1.5%; higher in burns patients (3-4%) and multi-trauma patients.
diagnosisOverview: Acute cholecystitis is diagnosed by combining clinical findings, laboratory tests, and imaging in accordance with the Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18). The TG18 diagnostic criteria require one local sign (Murphy's sign or RUQ mass, pain, or tenderness) AND one systemic sign (fever, or elevated CRP or leukocytosis), confirmed by imaging showing cholecystitis features. Laboratory investigations include full blood count (leukocytosis with neutrophilia in approximately 75-80% of cases), CRP (elevated in most cases, usually above 30 mg/L), and liver function tests — mildly elevated ALT, AST, alkaline phosphatase, and bilirubin suggest concomitant choledocholithiasis or Mirizzi syndrome. Elevated bilirubin above 85 µmol/L, alkaline phosphatase above 200 U/L, or dilated common bile duct on imaging warrants MRCP or EUS to exclude choledocholithiasis before cholecystectomy. Abdominal ultrasound is the first-line imaging modality: findings include gallstones or sludge, gallbladder wall thickening above 4 mm, pericholecystic fluid, gallbladder distension, and sonographic Murphy's sign (tenderness directly over the gallbladder under the ultrasound probe) — sensitivity approximately 88%, specificity 80%. CT abdomen is preferred when ultrasound is equivocal, when complications are suspected, or when acalculous cholecystitis is the concern; CT is more sensitive for detecting pericholecystic abscess, gas in the gallbladder wall, and perforation. Hepatobiliary iminodiacetic acid (HIDA) scintigraphy has sensitivity of approximately 97% and specificity of 90% for cystic duct obstruction but is rarely needed in the acute setting. MRCP is used when bile duct stones are suspected before planned cholecystectomy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging: detects gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, distension, and sonographic Murphy's sign
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Gel applied to the upper abdomen; 15-20 minutes. The patient is asked to hold a breath while the probe is pressed over the gallbladder. Sensitivity approximately 88% for acute cholecystitis. Operator-dependent; bowel gas may limit views.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies leukocytosis (WBC typically 12,000-15,000/µL) and neutrophilia supporting acute inflammation; very high WBC above 18,000 or bandemia suggests complications
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; result in 30-60 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: C-reactive protein (CRP) and liver function tests (LFTs)
diagnosticTests.purpose: CRP above 3 mg/dL (30 mg/L) is a TG18 diagnostic criterion; LFTs identify concomitant bile duct obstruction or hepatic involvement
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw; LFTs result in 1-2 hours. ALT and bilirubin above twice the upper limit of normal suggest choledocholithiasis warranting MRCP before cholecystectomy.

diagnosticTests.test: CT abdomen and pelvis (with IV contrast)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms cholecystitis when ultrasound is equivocal; detects complications including pericholecystic abscess, perforation, emphysematous change, and choledocholithiasis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: IV contrast injection; 10-minute scan. CT is superior to ultrasound for detecting complications. Particularly useful in obese patients where ultrasound is technically limited.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive imaging of the biliary tree to detect common bile duct stones before cholecystectomy; avoids diagnostic ERCP and its complications
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-40 minutes in the MRI scanner; no contrast needed. Patient is asked to hold breath for several seconds during image acquisition. Results reported by a radiologist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identify causative organisms (most commonly E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus) in febrile patients with suspected cholangitis or septicemia; guides antibiotic de-escalation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two sets drawn from different venipuncture sites; results in 24-48 hours for growth identification, 48-72 hours for sensitivities. Positive in 10-20% of acute cholecystitis, higher in cholangitis.

treatmentOverview: Management of acute cholecystitis follows the Tokyo Guidelines 2018 severity grading. For Grade I (mild) cholecystitis, early laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72 hours of symptom onset is the recommended treatment — level 1A evidence from randomized trials (including the ACDC trial) shows that early surgery reduces total hospital stay, complications, and costs compared with delayed surgery at 6-8 weeks. IV antibiotics covering Gram-negative enteric organisms and anaerobes — typically piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g every 8 hours, or ceftriaxone 2 g daily plus metronidazole 500 mg three times daily — are started at diagnosis and can be discontinued 24 hours after uncomplicated cholecystectomy. For Grade II (moderate) cholecystitis, early surgery is also preferred if the patient's operative risk is acceptable; local complications (pericholecystic abscess) may require pre-operative drainage. For Grade III (severe) cholecystitis with organ dysfunction, gallbladder drainage by percutaneous cholecystostomy is safer than emergency cholecystectomy in poor surgical candidates, followed by interval cholecystectomy when organ function recovers. Percutaneous cholecystostomy — placement of a drain into the gallbladder under ultrasound or CT guidance — decompresses the gallbladder, allows bile culture, and is successful in approximately 80-90% of cases. Endoscopic ultrasound-guided gallbladder drainage (EUS-GBD) using a lumen-apposing metal stent (LAMS) is a newer minimally invasive alternative for poor surgical candidates. Concomitant choledocholithiasis (common bile duct stones) requires ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction before or during the same hospitalization as cholecystectomy. Common bile duct exploration at laparoscopy is an alternative in centers with expertise. Ursodeoxycholic acid 250-500 mg twice daily following cholecystectomy for recurrent pigment stones or for prevention in high-risk patients (post-bariatric surgery) reduces recurrence.

medicalTreatments.name: IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g every 8 hours; or cefazolin 2 g every 8 hours for mild disease)
medicalTreatments.description: Perioperative antibiotics covering enteric Gram-negative bacilli and anaerobes are standard for all grades of acute cholecystitis. For mild disease without sepsis, cefazolin monotherapy or co-amoxiclav is adequate. For moderate-to-severe disease, broader cover with piperacillin-tazobactam is preferred.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prophylactic perioperative antibiotics reduce post-cholecystectomy infectious complications by approximately 60-70%; in Grade III cholecystitis, antibiotics combined with drainage resolve fever in 70-80% within 48 hours

medicalTreatments.name: IV analgesics (NSAIDs — ketorolac 30 mg IV, or opioids — morphine 2-5 mg IV)
medicalTreatments.description: Adequate pain control is essential throughout hospitalization. NSAIDs provide effective analgesia and have potential benefits from reducing prostaglandin-mediated gallbladder wall inflammation. Opioid use does not worsen outcomes and is appropriate for severe pain.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NSAIDs and opioids achieve adequate pain relief (NRS reduction ≥3 points) in approximately 80-90% of patients; no evidence that opioids worsen biliary pain through sphincter of Oddi effects at clinical doses

medicalTreatments.name: IV fluids and nil-by-mouth (NPO) status
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous crystalloid hydration maintains intravascular volume and prepares the patient for surgery. NBM status reduces aspiration risk at anaesthesia. Nasogastric tube insertion is reserved for patients with severe vomiting or suspected ileus.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Supportive care component; no specific RCT data but universally incorporated in management pathways

medicalTreatments.name: Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA 250-500 mg twice daily for 6 months) for prevention in high-risk groups
medicalTreatments.description: UDCA reduces biliary cholesterol saturation and is used prophylactically after bariatric surgery and in patients at high risk of rapid stone formation. Not indicated for treatment of established cholecystitis but relevant to recurrence prevention.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces new gallstone formation by approximately 40% in bariatric surgery patients (Shiffman et al. Ann Intern Med 1995); limited benefit once cholecystitis is established

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy (early, within 72 hours)
surgicalOptions.description: Four-port laparoscopic approach under general anaesthesia. The gallbladder is dissected from the liver bed after critical view of safety is achieved, cystic artery and duct are clipped and divided, and the gallbladder is retrieved in a bag. Intraoperative cholangiography or laparoscopic ultrasound can be performed to exclude CBD stones.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Conversion to open in 10-15% for acute cholecystitis (higher than elective setting); overall complication rate 3-8%; bile duct injury rate approximately 0.3-0.5%; hospital stay 1-2 days; return to work 1-2 weeks

surgicalOptions.name: Delayed (interval) laparoscopic cholecystectomy (6-8 weeks after acute episode)
surgicalOptions.description: Historically preferred over early surgery, but multiple randomized trials and meta-analyses demonstrate that early surgery is superior. Delayed approach is still appropriate for patients with prohibitive acute surgical risk (Grade III), those with pericholecystic abscess responding to antibiotics, or in settings without surgical expertise available 24 hours.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Outcomes similar to early surgery when patient is appropriate; readmission for recurrent cholecystitis while awaiting interval surgery occurs in approximately 15-20% of patients in delayed management series

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous cholecystostomy (PCT)
surgicalOptions.description: Ultrasound or CT-guided placement of a drainage catheter into the gallbladder, typically under local anaesthesia and mild sedation. Drains infected bile, decompresses the gallbladder, and is definitive treatment in approximately 30-40% of elderly or critically ill patients who are unfit for surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Technical success >95%; clinical response (fever and leukocytosis resolution within 72 hours) in approximately 80-90%; cholecystectomy subsequently required in 60-70% of patients who are fit enough

surgicalOptions.name: EUS-guided gallbladder drainage (EUS-GBD) with lumen-apposing metal stent (LAMS)
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic ultrasound-guided placement of a LAMS between the gallbladder and duodenum or stomach, creating an internal fistula for gallbladder drainage. A minimally invasive alternative to PCT in poor surgical candidates, particularly in patients with coagulopathy or ascites that complicate percutaneous drainage.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Technical success approximately 90-95%; clinical success approximately 85-90%; less stent dislodgement and recurrence compared with PCT in non-surgical patients in small RCTs

whySeeSpecialist: Any patient with suspected acute cholecystitis requires emergency department assessment and urgent general surgery consultation for cholecystectomy planning. Gastroenterology input is needed when choledocholithiasis or cholangitis coexists — ERCP expertise is mandatory in these cases. Interventional radiology is required for percutaneous cholecystostomy in patients not fit for surgery.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a hospital with 24-hour laparoscopic surgical capability; early cholecystectomy within 72 hours requires around-the-clock surgical availability. For complicated cases (suspected CBD stones, Mirizzi syndrome, cholangiocarcinoma), a hepatobiliary surgeon at a tertiary center offers the best outcomes. ERCP should be performed by a high-volume endoscopist — CBD injury rate drops below 0.1% in centers performing more than 200 ERCPs per year.
hospitalCriteria: 24/7 laparoscopic surgical capability for early cholecystectomy within 72 hours
Biliary ERCP service for concomitant choledocholithiasis
Interventional radiology for percutaneous cholecystostomy in high-risk surgical patients
ICU capability for Grade III cholecystitis with organ dysfunction
MRCP or EUS for biliary imaging when ultrasound is insufficient
keyFacilities: General surgery units at district general and teaching hospitals with 24/7 laparoscopic capability
Hepatobiliary surgery centers for complex gallbladder disease and biliary reconstruction
Advanced endoscopy units for ERCP and EUS-guided gallbladder drainage
Interventional radiology departments for percutaneous cholecystostomy
preventionStrategies: Maintain a healthy weight with gradual weight loss (maximum 0.5-1.0 kg per week) — rapid weight loss faster than 1.5 kg per week sharply increases gallstone formation risk.
Take ursodeoxycholic acid 500-600 mg daily for 6 months after bariatric surgery — reduces new gallstone formation by approximately 40% during the rapid weight-loss phase.
Increase physical activity — physically active individuals have 20-25% lower gallstone incidence than sedentary people in prospective cohort studies.
Maintain a diet rich in fiber (vegetables, fruits, legumes) and low in refined carbohydrates and saturated fats — reduces gallstone formation risk by modifying bile composition.
Elective cholecystectomy for symptomatic gallstones (biliary colic) before acute cholecystitis develops — patients with symptomatic gallstones have a 10-15% per year risk of acute complications.

In ICU patients on TPN: encourage early enteral feeding when clinically feasible, and consider IV cholecystokinin or prokinetics to maintain gallbladder contractility and prevent sludge and acalculous cholecystitis.
lifestyleModifications: Post-cholecystectomy: resume a normal diet within days of laparoscopic surgery — bile duct function adapts to continuous bile flow without the gallbladder reservoir within 3-6 months in most patients.

Some patients experience loose stools or diarrhea after cholecystectomy (postcholecystectomy syndrome) from continuous bile secretion into the duodenum — a low-fat diet and cholestyramine (if bile-acid diarrhea) may be needed.
Maintain a healthy body weight to reduce recurrence risk in those treated non-operatively and to reduce cardiovascular and metabolic risks that co-occur with gallstone disease.
Reduce alcohol intake — heavy alcohol use increases risk of gallbladder stasis and sludge formation.
Attend post-discharge surgical follow-up at 4-6 weeks to review histopathology of the removed gallbladder — approximately 1-2% of cholecystectomy specimens contain incidental gallbladder carcinoma.
For non-operative management (percutaneous cholecystostomy): drain tube care, regular nursing reviews, and planning for interval cholecystectomy within 2-3 months.

recommended: High-fiber diet (vegetables, fruits, whole grains, legumes) — reduces cholesterol gallstone formation by improving bile composition and bowel transit
Plant sterols and stanols (fortified spreads, soy products) — reduce intestinal cholesterol absorption and bile cholesterol saturation
Regular moderate coffee consumption (2-3 cups per day) — associated with 25-30% lower gallstone risk in large prospective cohort studies
Olive oil and omega-3 rich foods (oily fish, flaxseed) — reduce biliary cholesterol secretion in metabolic studies
avoid: High-fat, high-cholesterol meals that trigger gallbladder contraction and biliary colic in gallstone carriers
Refined carbohydrates and sugars — increase biliary cholesterol secretion and gallstone formation risk
Rapid weight loss diets faster than 1.5 kg per week — precipitates gallstone formation in 30-40% of dieters
Heavy alcohol — increases risk of gallbladder stasis, acalculous cholecystitis, and bile duct complications

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate-intensity aerobic exercise (150 minutes per week of brisk walking, cycling, or swimming) is associated with 20-25% lower gallstone incidence and is encouraged for primary prevention. After laparoscopic cholecystectomy: walking from day 1 post-surgery; driving after 1-2 weeks when comfortable; return to full exercise and sports at 3-4 weeks.
dailyManagement: During acute hospitalization: remain NPO, receive IV fluids and antibiotics, and undergo serial clinical assessment at least twice daily for fever resolution and abdominal examination changes.

For percutaneous cholecystostomy patients: drain care with daily output documentation; report sudden decrease in output (suggesting tube dislodgement or blockage) or change in drain contents to the care team.

After laparoscopic cholecystectomy: monitor wound sites for signs of infection; report fever above 38°C, increasing abdominal pain, jaundice, or dark urine — all may indicate a post-operative biliary complication.
Complete the full post-operative oral antibiotic course if prescribed for complicated cholecystitis.
Monitor for symptoms of bile leak post-cholecystectomy (increasing abdominal pain, fever, and bile-colored drain output) and seek immediate medical attention.
Follow-up with the surgical team at 4-6 weeks post-discharge for wound review, histopathology results, and liver function testing if bilirubin was elevated at admission.
prognosis: Uncomplicated acute calculous cholecystitis treated by early laparoscopic cholecystectomy has an excellent prognosis: in-hospital mortality below 0.5%, wound infection rate under 3%, and hospital stay of 1-2 days. Return to full activity occurs within 2-4 weeks in the majority. Complicated cholecystitis (Grade II-III) carries progressively higher mortality — approximately 1-5% for Grade II and 6-18% for Grade III in the TG18 cohort analysis, with outcomes strongly influenced by the patient's pre-existing organ function. Acalculous cholecystitis in ICU patients carries a reported mortality of 30-50%, primarily from the severity of the underlying illness. Patients treated non-operatively with PCT who subsequently have interval cholecystectomy have outcomes comparable to those who had early surgery, provided the interval procedure is performed within 2-3 months. Approximately 1-2% of cholecystectomy specimens show incidental gallbladder carcinoma — the 5-year survival is nearly 100% for stage I disease limited to the mucosa, falling to under 20% for T3 or greater disease. Postcholecystectomy syndrome (persistent dyspepsia, right upper quadrant discomfort after surgery) affects approximately 10-15% of patients and is usually manageable with dietary modification.
recoveryTimeline: Acute episode: fever and pain improve within 24-48 hours of antibiotics. Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72 hours: hospital stay 1-2 days; return to light activity in 1-2 weeks; full recovery at 3-4 weeks. PCT: drain typically removed after 4-6 weeks once a cholangiogram confirms no residual cystic duct obstruction; interval cholecystectomy planned at 2-3 months. Grade III cholecystitis: ICU stay variable depending on organ support required; interval cholecystectomy at 6-8 weeks after ICU discharge.
complications: Gallbladder empyema — frank pus filling the gallbladder lumen; presents with high fever, rigors, and worsening abdominal pain; requires urgent cholecystectomy or PCT.

Gangrenous cholecystitis — full-thickness gallbladder wall necrosis preceding perforation; found in approximately 15-20% of operated acute cholecystitis; higher conversion to open surgery and longer hospital stay.
Gallbladder perforation with bile peritonitis — free perforation releasing infected bile into the peritoneal cavity; mortality 5-15%; emergency surgery is mandatory.

Mirizzi syndrome — a large gallstone in the cystic duct or Hartmann's pouch compressing the common hepatic duct, causing obstructive jaundice; requires hepatobiliary surgical expertise for safe management.
Acute cholangitis — ascending biliary infection from concomitant choledocholithiasis, presenting with Charcot's triad (RUQ pain, fever, jaundice); requires urgent ERCP and biliary drainage.

Bilioenteric fistula — chronic pressure from a large gallstone erodes through the gallbladder wall into the duodenum or colon, occasionally causing gallstone ileus through obstruction of the terminal ileum.

supportResources.name: American College of Gastroenterology — Gallstones and Biliary Disease
supportResources.description: ACG patient education on gallstones, cholecystitis, symptoms, and treatment options.

supportResources.name: British Society of Gastroenterology — Gallstone Patient Information
supportResources.description: BSG-endorsed patient guide to gallstone disease, cholecystitis, and cholecystectomy.

supportResources.name: NHS — Cholecystitis
supportResources.description: NHS patient information on cholecystitis symptoms, diagnosis, and surgery.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute appendicitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Appendicitis causes right iliac fossa pain centered at McBurney's point, while cholecystitis causes right upper quadrant pain with positive Murphy's sign. Both cause fever and leukocytosis. Ultrasound readily distinguishes the two by identifying gallbladder inflammation versus an inflamed appendix in the RIF.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peptic ulcer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Perforated peptic ulcer causes sudden severe epigastric pain with board-like rigidity and free air on erect chest X-ray, whereas cholecystitis typically has a more gradual onset without free peritoneal air. Liver function tests and an erect CXR distinguish the two in most cases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gallstones (biliary colic)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Biliary colic is intermittent RUQ pain from transient cystic duct obstruction that resolves completely within 30 minutes to 6 hours without fever, leukocytosis, or Murphy's sign. Acute cholecystitis features persistent pain beyond 6 hours, fever, and elevated inflammatory markers — the transition from colic to established cholecystitis.

relatedConditions.name: Gallstones
relatedConditions.relevance: Gallstones are the direct cause of 90-95% of acute cholecystitis episodes; symptomatic gallstone disease is the substrate for the acute complication.

relatedConditions.name: Acute appendicitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The main differential diagnosis for right-sided acute abdominal pain; distinguished by pain location, Murphy's sign, and ultrasound findings.

relatedConditions.name: Peptic ulcer
relatedConditions.relevance: Both cause upper abdominal pain and are common acute surgical conditions; perforated peptic ulcer must be excluded in acute epigastric presentations.

relatedConditions.name: Liver abscess
relatedConditions.relevance: Hepatic abscess can present with right upper quadrant pain and fever, mimicking complicated cholecystitis; CT or ultrasound distinguishes the two.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of acute cholecystitis?
faqs.answer: Acute cholecystitis causes sudden severe right upper quadrant pain lasting more than 6 hours, fever, nausea, and vomiting. Positive Murphy's sign — pain and inspiratory arrest when pressing under the right rib margin — is the most specific clinical finding. Pain may radiate to the right shoulder tip. Simple biliary colic episodes typically precede the acute attack.

faqs.question: What is Murphy's sign in cholecystitis?
faqs.answer: Murphy's sign is elicited by pressing two fingers under the right costal margin while asking the patient to take a deep breath. As the inflamed gallbladder descends to meet the examiner's fingers, sudden pain causes the patient to stop breathing mid-inspiration. It has approximately 97% specificity for acute cholecystitis but is negative in one-third of cases.

faqs.question: How is acute cholecystitis treated?
faqs.answer: The standard treatment is early laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72 hours of symptom onset, combined with IV antibiotics. For patients too unwell for surgery, percutaneous cholecystostomy drains the gallbladder under imaging guidance. Early surgery compared with delayed surgery reduces total hospital stay and complication rates without increasing operative risk.

faqs.question: What is the difference between cholecystitis and gallstones?
faqs.answer: Gallstones are hardened deposits in the gallbladder that may cause no symptoms or intermittent biliary colic. Cholecystitis is acute inflammation of the gallbladder caused when a gallstone blocks the cystic duct. Biliary colic resolves within hours; cholecystitis persists with fever and rising inflammatory markers.

faqs.question: Is acute cholecystitis dangerous?
faqs.answer: Mild cholecystitis treated early has a mortality below 0.5%. Complications including gallbladder gangrene, perforation, and sepsis increase mortality to 6-18%. Acalculous cholecystitis in critically ill ICU patients carries 30-50% mortality from the severity of the underlying illness. Early diagnosis and treatment prevents dangerous complications.

faqs.question: How long does acute cholecystitis last without surgery?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, acute cholecystitis may resolve spontaneously in approximately 25-30% of cases over 7-10 days, but recurrence is extremely common and complication risk (gangrene, perforation) increases with time. IV antibiotics reduce fever within 24-48 hours, but antibiotics alone without cholecystectomy have a 15-25% recurrence rate within 30 days.

faqs.question: What does a cholecystitis ultrasound show?
faqs.answer: Ultrasound shows gallbladder wall thickening above 4 mm, pericholecystic fluid, gallbladder distension, and gallstones or sludge. The sonographic Murphy's sign (probe tenderness over the gallbladder) is highly specific. Ultrasound has approximately 88% sensitivity for acute cholecystitis and is the recommended first-line imaging investigation.

faqs.question: Can cholecystitis occur without gallstones?
faqs.answer: Yes. Acalculous cholecystitis accounts for 5-10% of cases and occurs in critically ill patients — those on TPN, after major surgery, or with burns and multi-trauma — from gallbladder ischemia and bile stasis without stones. It carries higher mortality (30-50%) than calculous disease due to the severity of the underlying condition.

faqs.question: What antibiotics are used for cholecystitis?
faqs.answer: For mild cholecystitis, cefazolin 2 g IV every 8 hours or co-amoxiclav covers the most common organisms. For moderate-to-severe disease, piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 8 hours or meropenem is used. Antibiotics target E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, and Bacteroides fragilis, the most frequent bile pathogens.

faqs.question: How quickly should cholecystitis be operated on?
faqs.answer: Tokyo Guidelines 2018 and multiple randomized trials recommend early laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72 hours of symptom onset for Grade I and Grade II cholecystitis. Waiting beyond 72-96 hours increases inflammation and adhesion formation, raising the conversion-to-open rate from approximately 10% to 30-40%.

faqs.question: What is the Tokyo grading for cholecystitis?
faqs.answer: Tokyo Guidelines 2018 classify acute cholecystitis into three grades: Grade I (mild — no organ dysfunction, responds to early surgery), Grade II (moderate — local complications such as pericholecystic abscess or empyema), and Grade III (severe — multi-organ dysfunction requiring ICU care and gallbladder drainage before surgery).

faqs.question: Can you eat after a cholecystectomy?
faqs.answer: Yes. A normal diet is usually resumed within 1-2 days after laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Some patients experience loose stools or mild diarrhea for weeks to months as the bowel adjusts to continuous bile flow without the gallbladder reservoir. A low-fat diet during the first few weeks eases this transition.

faqs.question: Who is at risk of acute cholecystitis?
faqs.answer: Women aged 40-70 have the highest risk, particularly those with obesity, prior pregnancies, or estrogen use. Other risk factors include Type 2 diabetes, rapid weight loss, and critical illness. The classic 'four Fs' mnemonic — fat, female, fertile, forty — captures the main demographic risk profile.

faqs.question: What is acalculous cholecystitis?
faqs.answer: Acalculous cholecystitis is acute gallbladder inflammation without gallstones, caused by ischemia and bile stasis in critically ill patients — those with major trauma, burns, post-cardiac surgery, or on total parenteral nutrition. Diagnosis by ultrasound or CT in the ICU. Treatment is percutaneous cholecystostomy rather than emergency surgery in most cases.

faqs.question: What is the long-term outlook after cholecystitis?
faqs.answer: After laparoscopic cholecystectomy, the long-term prognosis is excellent. The gallbladder is not essential to digestion, and most patients have no dietary restrictions after 3-6 months. Approximately 10-15% develop postcholecystectomy syndrome with mild ongoing dyspepsia or loose stools. Gallbladder cancer is found incidentally in 1-2% of specimens.
